note_data.title: Thermodynamics
keyPoints: **First law of thermodynamics**: The increase in internal energy of a system equals the sum of heat supplied to the system and work done on the system (ΔU = Q + W)
**Internal energy** is the sum of the random kinetic and potential energies of molecules in a system
**Specific heat capacity** is the energy required per unit mass to raise temperature by 1K (or 1°C)
**Specific latent heat of fusion** is the energy required per unit mass to change solid to liquid at constant temperature
**Specific latent heat of vaporization** is the energy required per unit mass to change liquid to gas at constant temperature
Work done by a gas during expansion equals the area under a pressure-volume graph
For an **ideal gas**: internal energy depends only on temperature and is directly proportional to absolute temperature
During **isothermal process**: temperature remains constant so ΔU = 0, therefore Q = -W
During **adiabatic process**: no heat transfer occurs so Q = 0, therefore ΔU = W
**Cyclic process**: system returns to initial state so net change in internal energy is zero

definitions.definition: The increase in internal energy of a system equals the sum of heat supplied to the system and work done on the system
The sum of the random kinetic and potential energies of molecules in a system
The energy required per unit mass to raise the temperature by 1K
The energy required per unit mass to change the state of a substance at constant temperature
A thermodynamic process in which temperature remains constant

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the law or definition in one clear sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the physical reason using 'because' - link energy changes to molecular behavior
Show all steps clearly. State the formula, substitute values, give final answer with correct units
Draw labeled axes, show correct shape of curve, mark key points and values

modelAnswer.question: A gas undergoes an isothermal expansion from volume 2.0 × 10⁻³ m³ to 6.0 × 10⁻³ m³. During this process, 450 J of heat is supplied to the gas. Calculate the work done by the gas and explain the change in internal energy.
modelAnswer.answer: For an isothermal process, temperature is constant, so change in internal energy ΔU = 0. Using first law: ΔU = Q + W, therefore 0 = 450 + W, so W = -450 J. The negative sign shows work is done by the gas (expansion). Since temperature is constant for an ideal gas, there is no change in internal energy because internal energy depends only on temperature.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Correct application of first law ΔU = Q + W
Mark 3: W = -450 J with correct sign
Mark 4: Explanation that internal energy unchanged because temperature constant

commonMistakes: Never confuse work done BY the gas (negative) with work done ON the gas (positive) - expansion means work BY gas
Students often forget that internal energy of ideal gas depends ONLY on temperature, not pressure or volume
Never say 'heat capacity' when you mean 'specific heat capacity' - must specify per unit mass
Students confuse latent heat of fusion with vaporization - fusion is solid→liquid, vaporization is liquid→gas
Never use ΔU = mcΔT during phase changes - temperature is constant so use ΔU = ml instead
examinerTips: First law of thermodynamics appears in almost every Paper 4 and frequently in Paper 2
Always check signs carefully: Q positive when heat supplied TO system, W negative when work done BY system
For P-V diagrams, clockwise cycles do net work (area), anticlockwise cycles have work done on them
When calculating work from P-V graphs, count squares carefully and state the scale used
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: P-V diagram showing isothermal (curved line), adiabatic (steeper curve), and constant pressure/volume processes. Work done equals area under curve. Label axes as pressure (Pa) vs volume (m³).
